title: Finding roots in python
text:
A temperature scale that never became popular was that proposed by the Austrian chemist Johan Sebastián Farbunglet. The reference points for that scale were 0 °FB (4 °C), the temperature below which he began to sneeze when chilled, and 1000 °FB (98°C), the temperature at which beer (which was his favorite drink) would boil.

Obtain an expression for converting from Celsius degrees to Farbunglet degrees and another expression for converting from Farbunglet degrees to Rankine degrees.
What is the temperature range of 10°FB in Celsius, Kelvin, Fahrenheit, and Rankine?.
Make a spreadsheet that does the conversions in section 1.
Program a function in Python that does the same thing.

title: Finding roots in python
text:
Solution
In the next figure, we can observe the relationships among popular scales of temperature (more scales al this link). Relatins among temperature scales are straight lines (see figure), so change for one escale to other is quiete easy.



Graphical representation of different scales of temprature

title: Finding roots in python
text:
Example
Relation of Celsius scale with others:




from Celsius
to Celsius




Kelvin
x °C ≘ (x+273.15) K
x K ≘ (x−273.15) °C


Fahrenheit
x °C ≘ (x·9/5+32) °F
x °F ≘ (x−32)·5/9 °C


Rankine
x °C ≘ (x+273.15)·9/5 °R
x °R ≘ (x−491.67)·5/9 °C



So, to transform degrees Celsious to degrees Farbunglet and viceversa:
\[
( x ºFB - 4 ) * 98 / 1000 = ( x ºC ) / 100
\]




from Celsius
to Celsius




Farbunglet
x °C ≘ x·10 + 4 °FB
x °FB ≘ (x−491.67)·10 °C
